slides.name: intro-wireless-networks
slides.script: Wireless networks let devices communicate without wires, using radio waves. This module explores what wireless networks are, why they matter, and the basic components involved. You'll learn how wireless technology connects us seamlessly in everyday life.
slides.title: Introduction to Wireless Networks

slides.name: wireless-networks-why-important
slides.script: Wireless networks are important because they provide mobility, flexibility, and ease of installation. They enable devices like smartphones and laptops to connect anywhere. This freedom powers modern communication, smart homes, and IoT devices that rely on wireless connectivity.
slides.title: Why Wireless Networks Matter

slides.name: wireless-network-components
slides.script: Wireless networks consist of key components: access points that broadcast signals, client devices that connect, and routers that manage traffic. These parts work together to create a wireless environment where data flows without cables.
slides.title: Key Components of Wireless Networks

slides.name: simple-wireless-connection-python
scripts: This Python script scans for Wi-Fi networks on Windows, Linux, and macOS. It detects the OS and runs the appropriate command.
On Linux we use nmcli. On Windows we use netsh. On macOS we use the airport command.
The output is decoded, parsed, and each network name is printed.
slides.title: Scan Wi-Fi Networks with Python

slides.name: wireless-standards-overview
slides.script: Wireless networks follow standards like IEEE 802.11, which define how devices communicate. Different versions such as 802.11b, g, n, and ac vary in speed and range. Understanding these standards helps you choose the right wireless technology for your needs.
slides.title: Wireless Networking Standards
points: Different versions: b, g, n, ac

slides.name: wifi-standard-comparison
slides.script: Let's compare popular Wi-Fi standards to see their differences in speed and range. This helps clarify why newer standards improve performance and support more devices simultaneously.
slides.title: Comparing Wi-Fi Standards

modules.title: Wireless Network Configuration and Security
modules.subtitle: Module 2: Setting Up and Securing Wireless Networks

slides.name: wireless-network-configuration
slides.script: Configuring a wireless network involves setting the SSID, choosing security protocols, and managing channels. Proper setup ensures reliable connections and prevents interference. We'll explore common configuration steps to get your network running smoothly.
slides.title: Wireless Network Configuration Basics

slides.name: wifi-security-protocols
slides.script: Wireless security protects your network from unauthorized access. Common protocols include WEP, WPA, and WPA2. WPA2 is currently the most secure widely used standard, encrypting data to keep communications private and safe.
slides.title: Wireless Security Protocols

slides.name: configure-wifi-ap-python
scripts: This Python example configures a Wi-Fi hotspot on Windows, Linux, and macOS. It detects the OS and runs the appropriate command.
On Linux we use nmcli. On Windows we use netsh. On macOS we use networksetup or system commands.
The SSID and password are set, and the hotspot is started.
slides.title: Configure Wireless Access Point with Python
slides.code:
# Detect OS and run appropriate hotspot command
import subprocess
import sys

ssid = 'MyNetwork'
password = 'SecurePass123'

if sys.platform == 'win32':
    # Windows: netsh wlan set hostednetwork
    subprocess.run(
        ['netsh', 'wlan', 'set', 'hostednetwork', 'mode=allow', f'ssid={ssid}', f'key={password}'],
        check=True
    )
    subprocess.run(['netsh', 'wlan', 'start', 'hostednetwork'], check=True)
elif sys.platform == 'darwin':
    # macOS: networksetup (requires admin)
    subprocess.run(
        ['networksetup', '-setairportnetwork', 'en0', ssid, password],
        capture_output=True
    )
else:
    # Linux: nmcli
    subprocess.run(
        ['nmcli', 'device', 'wifi', 'hotspot', 'ifname', 'wlan0', 'ssid', ssid, 'password', password],
        check=True
    )

print(f'Configured AP with SSID: {ssid}')

slides.name: wireless-channel-management
slides.script: Wireless channels are frequency bands that networks use to communicate. Choosing the right channel reduces interference from other networks nearby. Tools can scan channels to find the least crowded one, improving connection quality.
slides.title: Managing Wireless Channels

slides.name: scan-wireless-channels-python
scripts: This Python code scans Wi-Fi channels on Windows, Linux, and macOS. It detects the OS and runs the appropriate scan command.
On Linux we use iwlist. On Windows we use netsh. On macOS we use airport -s.
The output is decoded, parsed for channel info, and printed.
slides.title: Scan Wi-Fi Channels with Python

modules.title: Wireless Network Protocols and Data Transmission
modules.subtitle: Module 3: Understanding Protocols and How Data Moves Wirelessly

slides.name: wireless-protocols-overview
slides.script: Wireless protocols define rules for communication. They ensure data is sent and received correctly over the air. Examples include Wi-Fi protocols like 802.11 standards and Bluetooth. Understanding these helps you grasp how devices talk wirelessly.
slides.title: Wireless Network Protocols

slides.name: wireless-data-transmission-concepts
slides.script: Data travels wirelessly in packets, small chunks of information. Protocols control packet size, timing, and error checking. This organized flow keeps wireless communication efficient and reliable despite interference or obstacles.
slides.title: How Data Transmits Wirelessly

slides.name: send-receive-udp-python
scripts: This Python example sends data over a wireless network using UDP sockets. UDP works on Windows, Linux, and macOS.
We create a UDP socket and send a broadcast message to the network.
UDP is connectionless, making it fast for real-time wireless communication.
slides.title: Send and Receive Data with UDP in Python
slides.code:
# Creates UDP socket
import socket

# Sender: broadcast message to network
sender = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
message = b'Hello Wireless World'
dest = ('192.168.1.255', 5005)
sender.sendto(message, dest)
sender.close()

slides.name: receive-udp-python
slides.title: Receive UDP Messages in Python
slides.code:
# Binds to UDP port
import socket

receiver = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
receiver.bind(("0.0.0.0", 5005))

print("Listening for UDP messages...")
while True:
    data, addr = receiver.recvfrom(1024)
    print(f"Received from {addr}: {data.decode()}")
scripts: This Python script listens for UDP messages. It binds to a port and waits for incoming data.
When a message arrives, it prints the sender address and content.
This models how wireless devices receive broadcast messages.

slides.name: wireless-errors-and-corrections
slides.script: Wireless data can be corrupted by noise or interference. Protocols use error detection and correction techniques such as checksums and retransmissions to ensure data integrity. This keeps wireless communication reliable even in noisy environments.
slides.title: Error Handling in Wireless Networks

modules.title: Advanced Wireless Networking Concepts and IoT Integration
modules.subtitle: Module 4: Exploring Advanced Topics and Internet of Things

slides.name: mesh-networks-intro
slides.script: Mesh networks consist of multiple nodes that connect directly and dynamically. This creates a self-healing system where data routes through various paths, improving coverage and reliability compared to traditional wireless networks.
slides.title: Introduction to Mesh Networks

slides.name: mesh-network-python-example
scripts: This Python example simulates a mesh node communicating with neighbors. It uses UDP sockets, which work on all platforms.
A UDP socket listens for messages while a thread sends greetings to neighbor nodes.
This models how mesh nodes exchange data dynamically.
slides.title: Simulate Mesh Node Communication in Python
slides.code:
# Starts UDP socket for listening
import socket
import threading

neighbors = [('192.168.1.2', 5005), ('192.168.1.3', 5005)]
sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
sock.bind(('0.0.0.0', 5005))

def listen():
    while True:
        data, addr = sock.recvfrom(1024)
        print(f'Message from {addr}: {data.decode()}')

def send_to_neighbors(msg):
    for n in neighbors:
        sock.sendto(msg.encode(), n)

threading.Thread(target=listen, daemon=True).start()
send_to_neighbors('Hello from mesh node')

slides.name: iot-wireless-integration
slides.script: Wireless networks are essential for Internet of Things devices, which connect sensors and appliances. Protocols like Zigbee and LoRa enable low-power, long-range communication, allowing IoT systems to operate efficiently and reliably.
slides.title: Wireless Networks in IoT
points: Use protocols like Zigbee and LoRa

slides.name: zigbee-communication-python-example
scripts: This example sends a Zigbee message using the digi-xbee library. Serial port paths differ by OS: COM on Windows, ttyUSB on Linux, tty.usbserial on macOS.
We open the device, discover the remote node, and send a message.
This demonstrates how Python controls wireless IoT devices.
slides.title: Send Zigbee Message with Python
slides.code:
# Detect serial port path by OS
import sys
from digi.xbee.devices import XBeeDevice

if sys.platform == 'win32':
    PORT = 'COM3'
elif sys.platform == 'darwin':
    PORT = '/dev/tty.usbserial-0001'
else:
    PORT = '/dev/ttyUSB0'

BAUD_RATE = 9600
device = XBeeDevice(PORT, BAUD_RATE)
device.open()

remote_device = device.get_network().discover_device('0013A20040B41234')
if remote_device:
    device.send_data(remote_device, 'Hello Zigbee')
    print('Message sent')
device.close()

slides.name: advanced-wireless-security
slides.script: Advanced wireless security includes WPA3 and enterprise authentication methods. These offer stronger encryption and protect against modern threats, crucial for business and IoT environments where data privacy is vital.
slides.title: Advanced Wireless Security

slides.name: module-summary-wireless-advanced
slides.script: This module covered mesh networking, IoT wireless protocols, and advanced security. These topics demonstrate how wireless technology evolves to meet demanding applications, offering flexibility, scalability, and safety.
slides.title: Module Summary: Advanced Wireless Topics
